data.title: Snap-on: A Quality Business, Product And Investment Opportunity
data.description:
At the start of 2024, I believed that Snap-on (SNA) had all the right tools to deliver on long-term returns. Amidst a very strong track record, growing demand for tools and a relatively modest valuation, I was quite upbeat on the outlook for the business.

Since that period of time, shares have seen modest gains as organic growth was hard to come by last year, yet margins kept improving, as the same applies to the strength of the balance sheet. Amidst a strong balance sheet, modest valuation and strong dividend track record, there is much to like based on the sound long-term performance, even as current growth is a bit underwhelming.

Innovative Tools For Professionals

Founded back in 1920, Snap-on aims to make work easier for professionals which perform serious tasks. Driven by its brand strength, the company, over time, has grown to supply these tools to customers in over 100 countries across the globe.

Its 13,000 workers generated $4.7 billion in product sales last year as the company has built up a very impressive dividend track record, having paid out dividends without interruption or reduction since 1939. Of course, the company is well known for its namesake brands, but it runs many other brand lines as well.

A just over five billion revenue number (including financial service revenues) is split up across four segments. The largest of these is the Snap-on Tools Group business, which provides such tools to vehicle repair systems, with this segment responsible for 35% of sales. Repair Systems & Information Group is responsible for nearly a third of sales, supplying these tools to vehicle repair shop owners and managers.

The commercial and industrial group makes up just over a quarter of sales, providing supplies to professionals in critical industries. This is complemented by a small financial service revenue business, responsible for 7% of sales.

The company claims to benefit from long-term trends which include a growing vehicle fleet, cars on average getting older, with vehicles becoming more complex as well. Of course, accident rates have been moving up due to driver distraction and high traffic, as autonomous driving could be a negative for the business, of course.

The company has been employing a rapid continued improvements strategy since 2005 as operating margins from the product business have risen from 6% at that point in time to over 22% of sales right now.

Reading Into Expectations

Over the past decade, Snap-on has seen decent sales growth and revenues have grown from $3.3 billion to $4.7 billion, with cumulative revenue growth of more than 40% outpacing inflation by a modest degree. Amidst modest margin gains, the company has grown earnings per share from about $7 per share to $20 per share.

Frankly, the company has grown more into the valuation. A decade ago, shares had just enjoyed a solid momentum run, pushing shares up to levels around $150 per share. Shares traded around that mark until the pandemic, and thereafter rose in a spectacular fashion to levels around the $370 mark around the time of the US presidential election in the fall of last year. Ever since, shares have gradually come down to current levels around $320 per share.

In February of this year, Snap-on reported 2024 sales dead flat at $5.11 billion. Product sales were down half a percent to $4.71 billion, driven by a near a percent organic decline in sales. This minimal revenue decline was offset by growth in the financial service unit, which generated $401 million in sales. Operating earnings improved in a minimal fashion to $1.35 billion thanks to improving margins and growth in the high margin financial service business, with GAAP earnings up seventy-five cents to $19.51 per share.

The balance sheet requires some attention, but generally is very healthy. The company operates with over $1.3 billion in cash and equivalents as total contract and finance receivables stand near $2.5 billion, with regular debt standing at just $1.2 billion.

With 53 million shares trading at $320, the company is valued at $17.0 billion, at least equity of the business, with the company operating with a net cash position, I would argue. With earnings seen close to $20 per share, a 16 times earnings multiple given the history and balance sheet took quite reasonable, yet margins are very high on historical standards.

Stagnant Trends

Supported by the sound earnings results for 2024, the company announced a 15% hike in the dividend in November of last year, with dividends now paid out at a rate of $8.58 per share. This marks the 15th consecutive dividend hike, as the percentage hike was rather substantial here, representing a 2.7% dividend yield at current rates.

In April, Snap-on reported a 3.5% fall in first quarter sales to $1.14 billion, with organic sales down just over 2%, and a relatively strong dollar having a negative effects as well. This weighed a bit on earnings, with GAAP profits down forty cents to $4.51 per share. Last year's earnings benefited from a legal receipt to the tune of sixteen cents, with higher pension expenses hurting this year's earnings by nine cents.

While no formal guidance was released, the company believes that the business and end markets should be resilient in an uncertain period of time, with recent dollar weakness providing a tailwind as well.

Given the soft quarter to the year, earnings of $20 per share are not in the works this year, as flattish earnings from here yield an $18-$19 earnings estimate, for still a very reasonable 17-18 times earnings multiple. Moreover, investors receive a solid dividend, modest buybacks as the balance sheet remains in very good shape here.

What Now?

The truth is that Snap-on is quality, its business, its tools and long term (dividend) track record. With a reindustrialization of the US on the rise, that could be a driver for the business.

Besides its positioning, there is a reasonable valuation applied to the business, while the balance sheet is in a very strong shape, making that there is much to like about the business, perhaps too much of a premium positioning and pricing, as perhaps some selective M&A could drive the business further here.

For now, I actually still like what I see from a long-term perspective, even as the near term performance is lackluster (with recent organic growth declines reported). A softer dollar should be helpful in growing at least reported sales, with modest valuation and a strong balance sheet providing drivers for the business going forward upon sensible capital allocation decisions.

data.title: My Highest Conviction High-Yield Infrastructure Investment
data.description:
As I recently discussed, I love investing in high-yield infrastructure stocks (UTF). There are several reasons for this. First, infrastructure is benefiting from several macro tailwinds, including the AI boom, the need to modernize infrastructure in developed economies and build it in developing economies, and the growing demand for inflation-resistant and stable cash flowing assets to support the income needs of the growing number of retirees. Second, high-yield infrastructure stocks tend to be among the most dependable income machines given that the dividends are often supported by cash flows that are either highly contracted or regulated, making them very defensive in nature. Finally, infrastructure tends to be inflation resistant due to the real asset and critical nature of most quality infrastructure businesses as well as the fact that most infrastructure assets have inflation indexation and/or protections built into their contracts.

Given these strengths, infrastructure occupies the largest spot of all sectors in my dividend portfolio. Right now, my highest conviction high-yield infrastructure pick is Brookfield Renewable Partners (BEP). In this article, I will discuss why along with a few other honorable mentions.

My Highest Conviction High-Yield Infrastructure Pick

I last discussed BEP back in March. In that article, I detailed the investment thesis. Namely:

  • BEP issues a K-1 tax form, but does not generate any UBTI and is therefore suitable for holding in an IRA or 401k. It also has an economically equivalent C-Corp, known as Brookfield Renewable Corporation (BEPC).
  • BEP has been generating strong double-digit year-over-year FFO per unit growth and expects to continue doing so for the foreseeable future.
  • While market sentiment has been poor on BEP recently due to higher interest rates and negative headlines surrounding the renewable power sector, it is well-positioned to weather interest rate headwinds (more on that later) and is unlikely to suffer any material headwinds from the Trump administration's policies, with it stating on a recent earnings call:

while the renewable sector has traded down in the public markets on weaker sentiment stemming from the new US administration's announced executive orders and potential policy changes for renewables, the simple fact is that the fundamentals for energy have never been better. The low-cost renewable technologies that we have built our business on are the cheapest form of electricity production and are seeing greater demand than ever before. As a result, we believe that low-cost renewables, which are readily available to deploy, will play a leading role in the requirements for any and all increases in generation capacity that we are already seeing unfold.

  • Their huge PPA with Microsoft (MSFT) to power its AI ambitions along with expected further similar deals with other hyperscalers adds considerable strength to the growth thesis.
  • The balance sheet is very strong, as evidenced by the BBB+ credit rating.
  • It has a huge development pipeline (200,000 megawatts) with expectations that it will accelerate the pace at which it brings these projects online each year moving forward.
  • The asset portfolio is well-diversified by geography and technology, and it is protected against inflation and recession with its highly contracted and inflation-indexed cash flows.

Since then, BEP has done ok, generating 4% in total returns, but still remains deeply undervalued in my view.

In fact, my conviction in its value proposition has only strengthened with the release of BEP's Q1 results. Its FFO per unit increased by 7% year-over-year, and it continues to target 10% plus FFO per unit growth in 2025, similar to what it generated in 2024. Drilling underneath the headline numbers, there was a lot of good news that points to accelerating growth rates moving forward. First of all, management stated that they see the impact of the tariff announcements as not being material to the business, since they have already diversified their supply chains away from China for importing essential materials and items into the U.S. markets due to previous tariffs.

Management also offered a very promising update on its MSFT agreement, as they stated on the earnings call:

We also progressed the delivery of projects to Microsoft under our renewable energy framework agreement and continue to view the initial 10.5 gigawatts scoped into the agreement as the minimum we will contract under the framework. We expect to continue to partner with global technology players on both a project-by-project basis and via larger framework agreements given the persistence of the supply-demand imbalance we are seeing globally...If anything, we probably have more confidence in our arrangement with Microsoft than we did 12 or 15 months ago. As their needs are changing, we like to think there is no platform around the world that is better equipped to adjust with them. And as such, we expect our partnership to only grow and increase beyond what was originally announced.

On top of that, they continue to expect additional deals with hyperscalers and even stated that inbounds and interest have increased since the deal was made with Microsoft, with the interest particularly accelerating in recent months. Management even said,

as we sit here today, we would suggest that it is probably far more likely than not that we would execute similar-type agreements within 2025.

This could be a major tailwind both for the company’s growth pipeline and, even more so, for the stock price, since it would indicate resilient growth for the business and continue to advance management’s narrative that they are a major way to play the AI infrastructure boom that the market has yet to give them much credit for.

The company’s development pipeline is also growing rapidly, which is another major tailwind for growth. For example, in Q1, they brought 800 megawatts of renewable energy capacity online and expect to bring on a whopping 8 gigawatts throughout this year, which indicates that, in the coming quarters, their pace of bringing new capacity online will be accelerating dramatically, thereby leading to stronger FFO per unit growth in the coming quarters relative to Q1, simply from the development pipeline alone. To show how fast this development pipeline has scaled, if they achieve their guidance for the year, they will have doubled their run rate of commissioning capacity in just three years. For a company that is engaged in many other activities, including buy-and-hold opportunistic capital recycling, and is paying out a hefty distribution to investors, that sort of development pipeline growth rate is phenomenal.

Another major tailwind for the business is its hydro portfolio, which has long been the crown jewel of the company and appears to remain so today. This is due to the fact that hydrological conditions in North America are setting it up for a strong performance for the rest of the year, as well as the fact that it has 6,000 gigawatts of generation coming up for recontracting over the next half-decade. The company expects to recontract this capacity at materially higher rates, given where rates stand today, which will not only deliver a strong EBITDA bump to the business but also provide additional capacity for up-financings to free up further proceeds for reinvestment into new growth opportunities.

Management also pointed to the Westinghouse business as a growth driver in the coming months and years, as it sees enormous tailwinds from AI for that business segment:

we're thrilled with our exposure to Westinghouse. The tailwinds for nuclear get stronger day by day, just one, given the electricity demands around the world and nuclear's ability to provide clean dispatchable baseload power at scale. The added benefit that should not go unrecognized is in all the headlines, nuclear is a key focus of the new U.S. administration. And as the leading technology globally in the space, but of U.S. origin, Westinghouse is certainly the beneficiary of that. In terms - the one thing I would highlight is the tremendous demand and growth we are seeing in terms of nuclear isn't actually even showing up in Westinghouse's financials yet. The financials are performing well. I would say they're absolutely tracking to underwriting. But the thing that's exciting for us is the orders that are coming in are certainly above what we initially expected. And that creates a very positive outlook for financials in future periods. That will take a few years to play out, but it gives us incredible confidence for the foundation and outlook for that business.

Last but not least, its balance sheet remains in very strong shape, with approximately $4.5 billion in total available liquidity. The company continues to access the debt markets at highly attractive rates, as evidenced by its recent issuance of 450 million Canadian dollars in 10-year notes at a very favorable 4.54% interest rate. Additionally, it is generating significant proceeds from asset sales at strong returns. Management noted that the market for asset recycling remains robust and that their pipeline of potential asset sales is large. This will provide additional capital to fund their impressive growth pipeline. Furthermore, BEP’s strong liquidity position has enabled it to repurchase units opportunistically on the recent dip, with management stating,

We have been active repurchasing our units at current trading levels, as we see this as an accretive use of capital.

When you combine all of those strengths with the attractive total return proposition - a 6.3% yield, a 12.9x P/AFFO compared to its 3-year average P/AFFO of 16.5x, and a 10% expected AFFO per unit CAGR through 2029 - BEP looks like a very attractive risk-adjusted investment right now.

Investor Takeaway

While there are many other attractive and high-quality dividend-paying energy infrastructure investments out there, they simply fail to match up to BEP's combination of yield, growth, and balance sheet strength. For example, Clearway Energy (CWEN)(CWEN.A) is likely only going to deliver a mid-to-high single-digits CAFD per share CAGR through 2029 based on management's guidance, while offering a 5.9% dividend yield that does not match BEP's. Furthermore, it is not even investment grade, whereas BEP has a BBB+ credit rating. Enterprise Products Partners (EPD) is another stellar blue chip energy infrastructure investment that I own and like a lot, which has a slightly better credit rating (A-) and a slightly better yield of 7.1%. However, its expected growth rate through 2029 on a Distributable Cash Flow per-unit basis is just 4.9% (roughly half of BEP's):

Finally, BEP's Brookfield infrastructure peer (BIP)(BIPC) has similar growth potential based on management guidance, but its yield is materially lower at just 5.3%. As a result, while I really like each of these alternatives and even own some of them, BEP remains my highest conviction high-yield infrastructure pick at High Yield Investor.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: Big Tech, Big Rulings, Big Rebounds
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Germany weighs 10% tax on internet giants like Google, Meta. (00:25) Federal appeals court issues stay on Trump tariffs ruling. (01:30) Red Robin Gourmet Burgers (RRGB) swings to a profit launching shares 55% higher after-hours. (02:38)

This is an abridged transcript.

The German government is drafting a legislative proposal to impose a 10% tax on internet giants such as Google (NASDAQ:GOOG) (NASDAQ:GOOGL) and Meta (NASDAQ:META), Culture Minister Wolfram Weimer told local magazine Stern.

"Monopoly-like structures have developed that not only restrict competition, but also over-concentrate media power, putting media diversity at risk," Weimer said.

"On the other hand, these corporations do billions of dollars in business with very high margins and profit enormously from our country's media and cultural output, as well as its infrastructure. But they pay hardly any taxes, invest too little, and give far too little back to society."

Weimer said the draft bill could be based on the Austrian model. Austria in 2020 introduced a 5% tax on online advertising revenue for large digital platform operators.

The minister also invited Google (GOOG (NASDAQ:GOOGL) management and key industry representatives to meetings at the Federal Chancellery to discuss alternatives, including voluntary commitments.

The European Commission had considered digital taxes on Big Tech to raise funds to repay its €350B post-Covid debt, Politico reported earlier this month, although this was opposed by countries such as Italy and Germany.

And just like that.

No, this is not about the new season of a show with that name on Max. But, I am referring to how in less than 24 hours there was a ruling, we reported on it, our top story on Wall Street Breakfast Thursday. We followed up on Wall Street Lunch with Kim Khan. And only hours later, a federal appeals court issued a stay on that ruling. By now you know the ruling to which I'm referring.

The U.S. Court of Appeals for the Federal Circuit on Thursday issued an administrative stay blocking a ruling from a lower court on Wednesday that struck down "reciprocal" tariffs imposed by President Trump in April.

That court, the U.S. Court of International Trade, ruled that Trump had exceeded his presidential authority.

The appellate court asked companies opposed to the tariffs to file a brief with their arguments before June 5, The Wall Street Journal reported. The Department of Justice has been given until June 9 to respond to that brief.

Earlier Thursday, the Justice Department asked the Federal Circuit to stay the lower court's judgment as it pursues an appeal.

Meanwhile, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said the two leaders need to weigh in to bring home a deal between the U.S. and China.

Red Robin (NASDAQ:RRGB) is up 30% in premarket action.

The restaurant chain’s ambitious plan to turn the business around continues to impress investors as it swung to a profit in the first quarter, doubled adjusted EBITDA from last year, lowered labor costs, and cut its debt by nearly $18M.

The results overshadowed lowered guidance for FY25 and expectations for comparable restaurant revenue to decline by 3%, launching shares more than 55% higher in Thursday’s after-hours trading.

“There is still more work to be done as we continue the comeback journey of this beloved brand and capture the significant opportunity in front of us," CEO David Pace said in a statement.

Get a more detailed view of Red Robin’s earnings numbers in this article.

What’s Trending on Seeking Alpha:

Costco outpaces Walmart and Target again in the U.S.

SEC ends legal battle with top crypto exchange Binance

Nvidia, Dell to provide new supercomputer to US Department of Energy

Catalyst watch:

  • The five-day ASCO Conference will begin. The major oncology conference will include a flurry of abstracts and poster presentations that could impact share prices in the healthcare sector.

  • EA Sports F1 25 will be released by Electronic Arts (EA) for PlayStation 5, Xbox Series X|S, and PC.

Now let’s take a look at the markets ahead of the opening bell. Dow, S&P and Nasdaq futures are flat. Crude oil is up 0.7% at $61/barrel. Bitcoin is down 0.8% at $104,000. Gold is down 0.6% at $3,297.

The FTSE 100 is up 0.8% and the DAX is up 1%.

The biggest movers for the day premarket: The Gap (NYSE:GAP) -15% – Shares tumbled as investors reacted to the company’s FY2025 guidance, which notably excluded the potential impact of proposed tariffs.

On today’s economic calendar:

  • 8:30 am Personal Income and Outlays

  • 9:45 am Chicago PMI

  • 10:00 am Consumer Sentiment

  • 4:45 pm San Francisco Federal Reserve Bank President Mary Daly will participate in panel before virtual Reagan National Economic Forum.

  • 7:30 pm Chicago Federal Reserve Bank President Austan Goolsbee will participate in a moderated Q&A before"The Interview Show with Mark Bazer"

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Super Micro Computer: A Golden AI Play With Deep Discount
data.description:
Executive Summary

It appears that I was excessively optimistic when I called Super Micro Computer's (NASDAQ:SMCI) 10-K release an "Inflection Point" because the stock is currently much cheaper compared to late February. The stock is currently significantly undervalued because it looks like investors incorporate unfairly pessimistic growth assumptions. At the same time, SMCI's financial performance remains strong and Dell's yesterday's earnings release screams that the demand for AI servers continues soaring. Therefore, reiterating STRONG BUY is fair.

SMCI Analysis

The most fresh positive development for SMCI is its competitor's, Dell's, yesterday earnings release. Dell's Q1 performance was strong with quite optimistic guidance and the linchpin of this strength is the AI demand. Like SMCI, Dell is also a prominent player in the AI server's market. In Dell's yesterday earnings release, the company's CEO emphasized "unprecedented demand for our AI-optimized servers". This is certainly a big positive factor for SMCI because the company has a proven record of its ability to capitalize on AI tailwinds because SMCI's revenue growth trajectory demonstrated over the last three years is very similar to Nvidia's (NVDA) soaring sales.

I am comparing SMCI's revenue growth trajectory with Nvidia's because we all know that the GPU giant is considered to be by far the main AI winner due to its dominant positioning in high-performance processors. Therefore, using Nvidia's growth rate as a benchmark is a very useful exercise for SMCI's fundamental analysis.

With that being said, SMCI's ability to capitalize on AI tailwinds is robust. In addition, recent developments suggest that the company's firm positioning in the secular AI shift is still solid. SMCI continues investing resources and efforts in fortifying its technological bond with Nvidia. The recent COMPUTEX 2025 event once again demonstrated the strong partnership between SMCI and NVDA and the warm personal relationship between Charles Liang and Jensen Huang as Nvidia's CEO was featured as a special guest during SMCI's Keynote.

In addition, SMCI recently announced that it now accepts orders on portfolio of more than 20 systems compatible with the new Nvidia RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell Server Edition GPUs. This is a clear indication of SMCI's commitment to further deepen its strategic relationships with Nvidia. It is a vital step in fortifying SMCI's important place in the AI revolution as Nvidia's fresh earnings release with an upbeat guidance despite trade war headwinds, demonstrated that this company's moat in GPUs is still intact. Therefore, having a deep strategic partnership with Nvidia is a solid strategic advantage of SMCI.

To mitigate concentration risks by relying heavily only on the Nvidia partnership, SMCI also works hard in other directions. The company's R&D budget appears to be quite efficient because SMCI consistently rolls out improvements to its offerings. In addition, SMCI builds strong relationships with other large semiconductor players as well. During May, the company also announced that its upgraded server solutions powered by AMD EPYC 4005 series processors delivered best-in-class cost and density per server. Since AMD is the world's number two player in AI chips, having a synergetic technological compatibility with its offerings is another strategic strength for SMCI.

As a result, it is not surprising that SMCI is a compelling partner for companies who seek opportunities to deliver cloud and AI solutions. The recent announcement of a $20 billion partnership with Saudi-based company DataVolt is a large development that underscores SMCI's reputation as a technologically advanced provider of AI server solutions.

And this strength is not just something one-off or seasonal but a robust secular trend. The AI revolution appears to be in its early innings as we see that industry growth forecasts are very aggressive. According to Precedence Research, the AI servers market is expected to compound with a 27.6% average growth rate over the next ten years. With strong AI tailwinds behind the back and SMCI's robust strategic positioning, the company is well-prepared to drive exponential shareholder value growth over the long run.

Valuation is a Gift

Based on forward valuation metrics, SMCI is extremely undervalued. For a company with a $21.5 billion TTM revenue which revenue is expected to double over the next few years, the current $25 billion market cap is very low. It means that the company's forward P/S ratio is projected to dip significantly below one over the last couple of years.

In addition, SMCI's EPS is also expected to demonstrate aggressive growth by almost doubling within the next three fiscal years. Therefore, in my FCF growth assumptions I can be confident in using aggressive growth assumptions. But before we delve into the future FCF trajectory, the discount rate should be calculated (10.31% based on the CAPM approach). WACC is not used due to quite low total debt compared to the market cap.

Now we can return to the FCF discussion. Calculating the year 1 assumption requires additional working, since SMCI's FY2024 FCF was deeply negative due to the aggressive capacity buildout to meet soaring demand. Therefore, I take FY2023 figures as a base, and apply this year's FCF margin to the recently reiterated FY2026 revenue guidance of $40 billion.

Therefore, the first-year assumption for SMCI's FCF is $2.792 billion. A very modest 10% FCF average growth rate for the model's horizon is applied, together with a conservative 3% growth rate into perpetuity. Combining all these assumptions together returns the fair value estimate of $51.7 billion, which means that the undervaluation is huge.

Tempering growth assumptions for the DCF model is my second scenario because the first scenario's upside potential might look unrealistic for some readers. Switching to a 2% growth rate into perpetuity and an excessively conservative 5% free cash flow growth rate for the next five years significantly undermines SMCI's fair value estimate. Nevertheless, the upside potential is still substantial (61% upside).

Potential Drawbacks

Dell's yesterday's strong earnings release is not only a good indicator for SMCI, but also has its drawbacks. This is an indication that there is another large player in the industry with a strong ability to capitalize on the soaring demand for AI hardware. Apart from Dell, the server and storage solutions industry is also represented by other large companies like Hewlett Packard Enterprise (HPE), and International Business Machines (IBM). All these companies are much larger in terms of their scale, meaning that the competition is fierce.

There is still an extremely high level of uncertainty regarding near term developments around policy of the U.S. regarding tariffs and international trade. While there were some positives, like trade deals with several large trade partners of the U.S., most of them have temporary nature, meaning that new adverse developments are possible.

Of course, I also have to mention that just a few months have passed since SMCI's 10-K release, which included the auditor's adverse opinion on SMCI's internal controls over its financial reporting process. As a former Big 4 auditor, I can confidently say that three months is not enough to resolve these issues. Moreover, material weaknesses spanned across multiple areas of internal controls, including IT general controls, segregation of duties and manual journal entries. Therefore, I recommend investors keep an eye over developments in this area because no news about resolving these issues might indicate the management's weak commitment to improving the company's financial reporting process.

The Verdict

The demand for AI servers keeps soaring, which is the major positive catalyst for SMCI. The company has a proven record of its ability to capitalize on AI tailwinds, which means that the gap between the current share price and its fair value is poised to narrow soon. STRONG BUY remains a fair rating for this deeply undervalued stock.

data.title: Size Matters, And UnitedHealth Is BIG: Initiating The Stock With A Buy
data.description:
UnitedHealth Group (NYSE:UNH) is under fire from all sides. The biggest company in the insurance industry, controlling 15% of the U.S. healthcare insurance market and serving over 29 million Americans, finds itself at the center of escalating scrutiny and investor concern. I am initiating the stock with a strong buy after the recent selloff, down 29% over the past month, which presents an attractive entry point for long-term investors, in my opinion. I’ve been watching the stock closely ever since tragedy struck its leadership last year, and now, I think the stock has bled enough since its 52-week high of $630 and its April highs of $600, currently trading at $295 per share.

The stock took a beating, to say the least, and while a stock pulling back over 50% over the past six months should raise fundamental concerns, I know investor panic when I see it. The downturn began when UNH warned of rising medical expenses and issued a cautious 2025 earnings forecast of around $30 per share. The company reiterated its full-year forecast in January, but by Q1 2025, in mid-April, it was forced to revise its guidance down to a range of $24.65–$25.15 per share, from the previously projected $28.15 to $28.65. Then came a rare and unsettling move: earlier in May, the company said its CEO, Andrew Witty, resigned due to personal reasons, and the company pulled its FY25 outlook completely, sending shares down over 18% to a four-year low, as seen below.

As if the financial pressure weren’t enough, Witty’s sudden resignation was the cherry on top. The board brought back Stephen Hemsley, who was the company’s CEO from 2006 to 2017, to restore confidence, but the market read it as a reactive move, not a vote of long-term stability. In my view, the selloff has now gone too far, and the stock has been de-risked enough since April as UNH shed more than 50% of its market value, wiping out over half its market cap. UNH has refuted the recent news report by WSJ, but the market isn’t buying that, which is exactly why I am.

A trip down memory lane:

Before all this, the last time we the stock traded above $600 was in December of last year, just before the CEO of the company’s medical insurance division, Brian Thompson, was shot dead in Manhattan. The incident stoked public outrage and amplified scrutiny of the healthcare insurance industry; UNH was at the forefront of it, and the stock tumbled below $500 and has struggled to recover since.

Over the past five years, the stock has been down around 16%, underperforming the S&P 500 and competitors. Elevance Health (ELV) is up 17%, and CVS Health (CVS) is down around 10%. Yet, when you look under the hood, UNH delivered the strongest growth: revenue jumped 55.6%, outpacing Elevance’s 45% and CVS’s 38.7%. Despite the market's current sentiment, I view UNH as the most compelling name in the group, especially now, as the company’s fundamentals remain solid, and its diversified model continues to scale through Optum. The latter is firing on multiple cylinders, with pharmacy services leading the charge (Optum Rx revenue came in at $35.1 billion for 1Q25, backed by new clients and expanded relationships with existing clients), and care delivery expanding patient volumes; the company currently expects “to have about 5.4 million value-based care patients" by the end of 2025. Optum Insight generated $4.6 billion in revenue during the quarter, but I’m more interested in the launch of new AI-powered claims processing tools, aimed at increasing productivity by over 20% for revenue cycle management customers, according to management. UNH’s Medicare Advantage business remains on track to serve an additional 800,000 people this year, while Optum Health is to add 650,000 net new patients during the same period, according to the earnings call. The combination of diverse revenue streams, deep healthcare integration, and AI-driven tools positions UNH as a force to be reckoned with, in my opinion.

As for the valuation, it’s attractive to say the least, after the recent sell-off. Investors de-risked UNH, and I think the stock presents an attractive entry point at current levels. I think the market reaction has been disconnected from the company’s long-term earnings power, operational scale, and proven ability to navigate through crises. With insider buying picking up and valuation multiples at decade lows, I see this dip as an opportunity, not a warning. Forward PE currently stands at 12.9, below the sector median at 16.9 and the five-year average of 20.6. Forward EV/Sales currently stands at 0.7, also below the sector’s median at 3.04 and the five-year average at 1.4. I don’t think we’ll be seeing these low multiples in the near future, and I think the worst is now behind us.

What’s next?

While my buy call is mainly on attractive valuation, aka telling investors to buy the weakness, I also believe UNH’s diversified business model, driven by Optum's growing presence in data, pharmacy services, and value-based care, provides a solid foundation for long-term earnings prospects. Despite recent stock underperformance, the fundamentals remain strong, and Optum's scale and innovation give UNH a structural edge in an increasingly cost-conscious and tech-driven healthcare environment.

data.title: SBA Communications: Hold Based On Technological Risk
data.description:
Summary

SBA Communications (NASDAQ:SBAC) is an owner & operator of wireless communications towers in the United States, Central America, South America, Canada, and Africa. With approximately 75% of its revenues generated in the U.S., it leans heavily on the “big three” cellular providers, which in turn generate consistent, stable revenues for the company through site leasing & maintenance. But with a strong business model comes inherent competition, as other independent players seek to grow market share or disrupt the tower communications industry with technological capabilities. Based on historical multiples, SBAC is trading cheaply, and management’s recent quarterly report signals a strong 2025. But despite any recent outperformance, SBAC is a “hold”.

Background: Tower Leasing in a Competitive Landscape

SBA Communications owns tower properties across the western hemisphere, and generates revenues through tower lease agreements. Most of its revenues are sourced from the United States, and roughly 25% of its 2024 sales originated in South America, Central America, Canada, Africa. The big three cellular providers – T-Mobile, AT&T, and Verizon Wireless – account for over 66% of its U.S. revenues. According to the most recent Annual Report, the company strategy involves owning or maintaining control of the property in which the cell towers are built and maintained, carrying more than one tenant within each tower, and issuing long-term leases (5 to 15 years) to providers in order to generate recurring revenue. On average, SBAC owns or controls tower properties with a 36-year term. They then turn around and lease the towers to wireless providers – usually with rent escalators in the contract. With a lessor-lessee structure, the business model is somewhat resistant to economic cyclicality, providing investor cover during economic downturns. Given that streaming demand has increased substantially over the years and is projected to reach 473 exabytes per month by 2030, the revenues are predictable, making for a good REIT investment.

Because the business model is somewhat insulated from macroeconomic factors, it draws competition from other independent players big and small. Consider a snippet from page five of the annual report, that outlines five different competitors who can intrude on SBA’s success:

“In the U.S., our primary competitors for our site leasing activities are (1) large independent tower companies including American Tower Corporation and Crown Castle International; (2) a number of regional independent tower owners; (3) wireless service providers that own and operate their own towers and lease […] antenna space to other providers; (4) owners and operators of alternative facilities such as rooftops […]; and (5) owners and operators of alternative wireless technology systems and architectures.”

Based on the text above, one can deduce that there aren’t many barriers to entry in the market. Hypothetically, we can imagine a large cellular tower owned & operated by SBA, sending signals to another tower several kilometers away on a farm property or commercial rooftop, leased out by the landowner. Alternatively, cellular providers may seek to develop their own network infrastructure: a possible outcome we may see with Boost Mobile, for example (a situation we will discuss shortly). Thirdly, while spectrum development and increased mobile data usage will serve SBAC well, there are other technologies (i.e., satellite communications) that could infringe upon the use of cellular towers. That said, the business model will remain compelling over the long term, but the investment could be deemed “good, not great”.

First Quarter 2025

The first quarter 2025 report was a beat on both top and bottom line, due to integration of the Millicom acquisition and faster site closings. Along the same lines, the strength of the business model previously mentioned came through on the conference call, when CEO Brendan Cavanagh indicated little-to-no tariff impact through fiscal 2025:

“We have not experienced nor do we foresee any direct impacts from the current tariff policies. Our business continues to generate steady cash flow, and the underlying needs of our customers remain robust. As a result, we have significant confidence in our company and our future.”

Just in the small scrap of news above, we can infer both SBAC’s continued consistency, as well as the revenue generation from – for example: network infrastructure build inside & outside the U.S. – it is worth noting that – due to the international exposure and drawing roughly 30% of international revenues from Brazil alone – SBAC will continually face currency volatility in its earnings reports. In 2024, international site revenues increased 4.8% on a constant-currency basis, yet in dollar denominated terms dropped by almost 1%, with a $37 million loss attributed to foreign exchange fluctuations. That trend continued into Q1, 2025, with a $16 million hit to international sales. While SBA is a good investment in terms of the business model, any dollar strength will consequently put a dent on international growth. On the other hand, we can view dollar weakness as a positive for SBA. If the recent dollar weakness exacerbates via flights to safe haven assets (i.e., gold or the Japanese Yen), then we’ll see a reverse of SBA’s currency impact into a tailwind, adding to the view that SBAC is a stable investment in the REIT space. It is a tariff-resistant, western hemisphere real estate play.

Two Glaring Risk Factors

Why, then, should SBAC be a “hold” as opposed to a “buy”? In my opinion, there are two risks that we should be keenly aware of: merger hangover, and technological advancements. Let’s take each of these in turn.

During the Q&A portion of the 2025 Q1 conference call, there was an interesting exchange regarding DISH network, the amount of FCC Spectrum rights they hold, and whether or not they will seek to lease the spectrum or utilize it via cell towers, implying a profitable impact on SBA. The CEO made the following remarks in response to a question about DISH leasing spectrum:

“In terms of just their broader commentary and feedback with us, we work pretty closely with them. They on the operation side are very clear about their desire to continue to pursue their standalone greenfield network. At this stage, obviously, things are much slower in terms of leasing activity with them. So we’re hopeful that changes. But at this stage, it’s really just meeting some very basic needs. There’s some basic upgrades going on. They are signing a few leases here and there, but it’s pretty small at this stage.”

There are two intertwined points to be made. First, EchoStar (SATS) (via its DISH ownership) is sitting on FCC spectrum and moving very slowly. Followers of DISH prior to its merger with EchoStar know that the company has sought to monetize its spectrum assets, but with little success. Second, EchoStar’s ownership of Boost Mobile is almost certainly the source of the “greenfield network” it is seeking to develop. A fourth cellular player in addition to the “big three” mentioned above would be beneficial for the tower business, but given the history of how DISH has handled its spectrum assets, we should be careful about assuming any tailwinds for SBAC down the road. The convoluted nature of EchoStar’s DISH merger & Boost Mobile development lend credence to the idea that wireless carrier mergers can end up hurting SBA Communications. Simply stated, with less wireless players comes consolidated usage on the antennae, and investors can’t count on a slow-moving Boost mobile as an added revenue stream.

There is another risk posed by the FCC spectrum debate…Introduction and advancement of new technologies presenting growth risks for the tower industry as a whole. This can be exemplified by Starlink’s recent fight for EchoStar spectrum, arguing that the company has not utilized its assets properly. Regardless of the validity behind the claim, we know Starlink’s success when it comes to providing wireless capabilities via satellite: no towers required. They even market the product as “a cellphone tower in space”. Needless to say, a cellular tower alternative like satellites could hurt the bottom line across the industry, SBAC included. Along the same lines, the “Risk Factors” section of the Annual Report highlights the technological risk that SBAC faces:

“…other technologies and architectures, such as WiFi, DAS, femtocells, other small cells, or satellite (such as low earth orbiting) and mesh transmission systems may, in the future, serve as substitutes for, or alternatives to, the traditional macro site communications architecture that is the basis of substantially all of our site leasing business” (2024 Annual Report, pp. 13)

Femtocells and complementary cells have a short range of a few hundred meters or less, compared to a macro tower that can reach a few miles. Therefore, the threat of small cells replacing SBA’s core business is relatively miniscule. Nevertheless, Moore’s Law still applies. It may very well be true that as technology advances, we will no longer need tower sites as much as we do today. I believe SBA Communications has a wonderfully consistent business, and represents a solid investment opportunity; however, merger hangover and technological advancement are two glaring risks to keep in mind.

Brief Valuation

Per Seeking Alpha, at $230/share, SBAC trades expensively against its peers. The EV/EBIT is high at 25x, and while that is below the median of 31x, we have to remember that as a REIT SBA will distribute 90% of its cash flow to shareholders. That said, we can look at the cash flows more so than the income statement. The forward price to funds from operations ratio stands at 19.6x, well above the 12.8x sector median. On the face of it SBAC looks expensive, yet if we narrow down to a price/cash from operations metric, specifically involving two competitors in American Tower Corporation (AMT) and Crown Castle Incorporated (CCI), then SBAC shares do not appear overpriced from a relative value standpoint:

It is perhaps more apt to say that the entire industry took a hit during 2023 and 2024, based primarily on interest rate movements. When rates went higher circa 2023, debt financing costs ballooned, and SBA’s customers pulled back on spending. Looking at five years of history in the chart above, it seems the businesses now trade at relatively low multiples. Similarly, SBA’s management recently announced a $1.5 billion share buyback in addition to affirming strong 2025 financials. Both are welcome signs that the downtrend may be over.

Conclusion

SBA Communications has a good business model. The company seeks to own tower properties for very long periods of time, while leasing out space to communications providers to generate stable, recurring revenue. Data networks have only gotten larger, as streaming over 5G has brought immense demand across the entire western hemisphere – that trend should continue, benefitting SBAC shareholders via its REIT structure. However, there are conspicuous risks to an investment that include technological advancements as well as merger hangover that dampens tower utilization. Namely, satellite architecture built by Starlink stands as a threat to the tower leasing business, and a slow-moving network adoption from EchoStar post-DISH merger will likely have little impact on SBA’s business. Given the cross currents of low historical valuation and increasing risks to the industry, SBA is a “hold”.

data.title: Southern European Banks Brace For Lending Income Hit As Faster Rate Cuts Loom
data.description:
Banks in Southern European markets face larger-than-expected falls in lending income in the coming quarters as the European Central Bank cuts rates to combat the threat of an economic slowdown caused by US tariffs.

Expectations of a faster easing of eurozone monetary policy rose following US President Donald Trump's April 2 announcement of tariffs on all US imports. Some analysts have suggested the eurozone's benchmark interest rate could fall to as low as 1% from the current 2.25%.

Lenders in Southern Europe — Italy, Spain, Portugal, Greece and Cyprus — are particularly sensitive to swings in interest rates due to the relatively large proportion of variable-rate loans on their balance sheets and their greater reliance on lending income to generate revenues. Many of the banks were among the biggest beneficiaries after interest rates rose sharply in 2022, enjoying significant boosts to profitability and shareholder payouts that drove a surge in their stock prices.

"Banks operating in Southern European economies have generally benefited most from the increase in interest rates in recent years," Andrea Costanzo, vice president of financial institutions at Morningstar DBRS, said in an interview. "These are also the banks that will probably suffer more when rates go down."

Losing interest

Net interest income (NII) — the difference between what banks earn from interest on loans and other assets and what they pay for deposits — is already falling at most eurozone banks. Aggregate NII at the eurozone's largest lenders fell more than 3% quarter over quarter and was down almost 2.5% annually in the first quarter of 2025, according to S&P Global Market Intelligence data.

Analysts expect further falls throughout 2025, with some Southern European lenders predicted to suffer further declines in 2026, data from Visible Alpha, part of Market Intelligence, shows. NII is expected to decrease annually in 2025 by an average of almost 5.7% among the large eurozone banks predicted to suffer drops this year.

Southern European banks make up 13 of the 21 large eurozone banks predicted to suffer NII declines in 2025. Only four Southern European lenders are forecast to enjoy increases to lending income.

Eurozone banks maintained their guidance for NII for 2025 when announcing first quarter results even as their expectations for terminal eurozone interest rates fell to about 1.5% from 2% in the previous quarter, Amal Shah, EY global banking senior analyst, said in an interview.

Still, further downward revisions to interest rate expectations could change that guidance. "Anything below 1.5%, potentially some impacts on NII do start to appear," Shah said.

A terminal rate of 1%, as some analysts are predicting, would be the "limit" beyond which the bloc's banks would suffer a significant hit to lending income, Filippo Alloatti, head of financials, credit, at global asset management firm Federated Hermes, said in an interview.

"Below 1% for whatever reason — recession, worse tariff outcome than expected, if we go to maybe 0.75% — then it becomes really tough," said Alloatti.

Trade tensions

The ECB cut rates by 25 basis points on April 23,bringing its benchmark deposit facility rate to 2.25%. This was the seventh 25-basis point reduction in rates since June 2024.

While ECB President Christine Lagarde reaffirmed its commitment to stabilize inflation sustainably at its 2% medium-term target when announcing the move, she warned of an economic outlook "clouded by exceptional uncertainty" that has "deteriorated owing to rising trade tensions."

The European Commission, the executive arm of the European Union, downgraded the eurozone economy's 2025 growth forecast to 0.9% on May 19, down 40 basis points from its last projection in November. The revision was largely due to the weaker and more uncertain global trade outlook caused by US tariffs, it said.

"The downside risks to growth suggest the ECB's policy path is relatively straightforward," Seema Shah, chief global strategist at Principal Asset Management, said via email. "If a recession becomes more probable, multiple additional cuts could follow."

Markets expect the ECB to cut borrowing costs by 25 basis points in June and by the same amount again in the second half of the year, to bring the deposit facility rate to 1.75%, according to Reuters data.

Southern European banks should be able to offset some of the damage lower rates would cause. Some of the region's banks, particularly Spanish lenders, have bolstered their long-term NII prospects in recent quarters by investing heavily in sovereign bonds at attractive rates.

Still favorable, albeit deteriorating, liquidity conditions are also allowing the banks to cut deposit costs as interest rates fall, further slowing the tightening of margins.

Lower interest rates should also boost demand for loans. Most Southern European economies have performed relatively well in recent quarters and should be largely insulated from the more direct negative impacts of US tariffs.

Italy exposed

Italy could be an exception, said Alloatti. Italy was the eurozone's third-largest exporter to the US in 2024, after Germany and Ireland, with outbound trade in goods worth almost €65 billion.

"It will be interesting to see whether those tariffs really push the eurozone as a whole into recession or just some economies," said Alloatti. "I think Italy, for sure, will slow down."

The eurozone currently faces a 10% tariff on most exports to the US following President Trump's decision to pause, for 90 days, the 20% tariffs originally announced on April 2. A 25% tariff on automotives, steel and aluminum remains. Trump last week threatened to impose a 50% tariff on EU goods from June 1, but subsequently delayed this to July 9.

A 10% tariff on most EU exports to the US would probably reduce eurozone GDP by about 0.2%, according to UK-based consultancy Capital Economics. But the damage could be greater due to the US administration's "flip-flopping," which is adding to the already high level of uncertainty, it added.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Hedged And Leveraged: Inside GDE
data.description:
The WisdomTree Efficient Gold Plus Equity Strategy Fund ETF (GDE) is an actively managed ETF that combines equity market returns while providing inflation protection through exposure to gold. Its core strategy combines investments in US large cap equities with gold futures contracts. The diversification benefits investors in risk-off periods through a relatively smoother capital appreciation path with lesser volatility.

Holdings Analysis

The current holdings indicate active selection of market cap weighted top US large caps, part of the S&P 500 or Nasdaq 100. The portfolio has a growth bias with a tilt towards technology. However, it will remain agile to autocorrect as and when sectoral rotation impacts larger markets. The core selling point of the fund is its capital efficiency. With 90% of funds invested in equities, cash investments in gold would have normally been 10%. However, using leverage of futures, GDE maintains ~90% notional exposure to gold. This takes the total exposure to capital to 180%. Usually, this leverage would be an area of concern, but given the hedge characteristics of gold and largely uncorrelated movements against equity, the proposition becomes unique and lucrative.

GDE utilizes gold futures contracts held through a wholly owned Cayman Islands subsidiary. The use of futures introduces some leverage and cost drags, including basis risk (difference between futures and spot prices), but allows for dynamic positioning. The fund periodically rebalances the equity and gold components to maintain target allocations and manage risk, although I did not see precise rebalancing frequency and details mentioned publicly.

The top holdings show 6.8% allocation to "WT EFF GOLD PLUS EQ STRAT PORT I". This is a subsidiary holding within the GDE ETF that provides exposure to gold futures as part of its strategy. Taking that out, the top ten holdings (accounting for a third of the total equity holding) look familiar. Even the sectoral allocation looks in line with S&P 500 broadly.

Performance

Comparing performance of investing $10k in GLD or SPY vs GDE is telling. The first thing I note is that while a combination of gold and equity should land somewhere in between the two in terms of performance, the leverage (or say capital efficiency) of using gold futures, amplifies gains. In around 3 years of existence, GDE has given an extraordinary return of 92%.

Over the past three years, both gold and equities have moved higher, though driven by different forces. Equities rebounded from a tough 2022 as inflation eased, and AI optimism boosted tech stocks, leading to strong gains in 2023–2025. Gold, after a flat 2022, surged amid rising geopolitical tensions, central bank buying, and fiscal concerns. The simultaneous rise reflects divergent yet complementary roles - equities benefiting from growth and innovation, while gold provided inflation protection and safe haven appeal.

However, there are two concerns that need to be allayed here. One, the GDE promise of lower volatility is hinging on the low correlation between gold and equities. The simultaneous up move shows the possibility of a simultaneous correction of stagnation in both as well. In such scenarios, GDE will show bigger drawdowns just like GDE outperformed both GLD and SPY on the up leg of the cycle. That threatens the low volatility case for GDE. Let us look into some sub periods over the data available for the past three years to understand how that plays out.

The period is a relatively flat market from early 2022 to late 2023. The leverage does seem to impact GDE in this market with GDE consistently trading below both gold and equities.

In the recent sell-off due to tariffs, a relatively short period to study a correcting market, the benefits of GDE come to the fore. Despite a broad equity sell off, GDE benefits from the expected hedge from gold.

So, the expected volatility drop of an approach like combining gold and equities, in theory, hinges on how uncorrelated they remain. While I don't mind correlation in a rising market, as we saw overall since inception, it is the drawdowns where the correlation should break. The drawdowns in a flat market are nor severe, and I can live with it for the higher leveraged gains on upsides.

Macro Context

The real risk lies in scenarios where both gold and equities decline simultaneously. While historically rare, such dual drawdowns typically occur during liquidity crises (e.g., March 2020), aggressive rate hike cycles, or periods of stagflation where real yields remain high and growth expectations fall. These are environments when investors sell both risk assets and defensive assets like gold to raise cash, resulting in a short term positive correlation on the downside.

Conversely, GDE thrives when both gold and equities rise together—a condition that’s been more common recently due to low real yields, slowing inflation and geopolitical uncertainty. Statistically, positive correlation between gold and equities tends to appear more often during euphoric rallies, while negative or low correlation is more typical in normal environments. In short, high correlation in stress scenarios (the risk for GDE) is rarer and more short-lived than high correlation on rallies (gain for GDE).

Investment Advice

2025 and maybe 20926 as well could be an environment dictated by tariffs, geopolitical tensions, economic uncertainty and higher for longer interest rates. GDE’s blend of equities and gold offers diversification benefits in such a scenario. Tariffs and geopolitical risks may boost gold as a safe haven while equities face volatility, supporting GDE’s strategy. Economic slowdowns might weaken equities but lift gold on inflation and safe-haven demand as well. The only material risk to the diversification thesis is higher for longer Fed rates and liquidity crunches that could cause both assets to fall simultaneously. As seen in a stagnating market and usually in stagflation scenarios, GDE could underperform.

Investors should view GDE as a growth vehicle rather than a low volatile instrument, as should have been in theory. GDE's higher leverage actually gives you greater growth over a long term, with hedge benefits on most occasions. When the hedge benefits disappear, which is rare and short-lived, investors should accumulate more. This strategy suits an investment horizon of at least 3 years to see visible positive results.

data.title: John Hancock Corporate Bond ETF Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Highlights

  • U.S. investment-grade corporate bonds rose in the first quarter.
  • The John Hancock Corporate Bond ETF (NYSEARCA:JHCB) fund performed in line with its benchmark, the Bloomberg U.S. Corporate Bond Index.
  • Yield curve positioning and sector allocation were additive to performance while individual security selection detracted from performance during the quarter.

Market review and outlook

U.S. investment-grade corporate bonds posted positive returns in the first quarter. Bonds in general sold off early amid strong economic data, including a better-than-expected jobs report in January. However, bond yields subsequently declined sharply as signs of slowing economic growth appeared, including a noteworthy decline in retail sales and the lowest consumer sentiment reading since November 2022. The U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed) held short-term interest rates steady throughout the quarter while lowering its economic projections and increasing its inflation forecasts. One negative factor for corporate bonds was shifting U.S. trade and tariff policies, which created considerable uncertainty and led to risk-off market sentiment during the latter half of the quarter.

For the quarter, bond yields declined across the board, with intermediate-term bond yields falling the most. Despite producing positive returns, investment grade corporate bonds underperformed the broader bond market due to the risk off market conditions late in the quarter.

Contributors and detractors

The fund performed in line with the benchmark in the first quarter. Yield curve positioning was additive to performance, with an overweight position in the intermediate maturity sector and an underweight position at the long end of the yield curve contributing positively to performance.

Sector allocation was also a positive contributor to relative performance, with an overweight position in the financials sector and an underweight position in the utilities sector adding the most value.

Individual security selection detracted from relative results in the first quarter. Noteworthy detractors included bonds issued by electric utility Pacific Gas & Electric Co. (PCG), pharmaceutical firm Viatris, Inc. (VTRS), and database software maker Oracle Corp. (ORCL) Leading contributors included financial services provider Deutsche Bank AG (DB), power producer NRG Energy, Inc. (NRG), and electric utility Vistra Corp. (VST)

Portfolio positioning

Bond market attention shifted from the Fed and its efforts to keep inflation in check to U.S. tariffs and their potential impact on domestic economic growth. We expect tariffs to remain a moving target going forward, so we continued to view them through a longer-term lens, which should allow us to take advantage of any market volatility related to changing U.S. trade policy. In corporate credit, yield spreads widened but remained tighter than historical averages, so we remained focused on individual security selection and adding incremental yield.

Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance cited. For the most recent month-end performance, visit Exchange-traded funds (ETFs) - Manulife John Hancock Investments.Market price and performance is calculated as follows: (i) for the time periods starting October 3, 2022, the NYSE Arca's Official Closing Price or, if it more accurately reflects market price at the time as of which NAV is calculated, the bid/ask midpoint as of that time and (ii) for time periods preceding October 3, 2022, the bid/ask midpoint at 4 P.M., Eastern time, when the NAV is typically calculated; your returns may differ if you traded shares at other times. NAV is calculated by dividing the total value of all the securities in the fund's portfolio plus cash, interest, and receivables, minus any liabilities, by the number of fund shares outstanding.

This commentary reflects the views of the portfolio managers named and is subject to change as market and other conditions warrant. No forecasts are guaranteed. This commentary is provided for informational purposes only and is not an endorsement of any security, mutual fund, sector, or index. The subadvisor, the advisor (John Hancock Investment Management LLC), and their affiliates, employees, and clients, may hold or trade the securities mentioned in this commentary. Diversification does not guarantee a profit or eliminate the risk of a loss.

What you should know before investing

Investing involves risks, including the potential loss of principal. There is no guarantee that a fund's investment strategy will be successful. Fixed-income investments are subject to interest-rate and credit risk; their value will normally decline as interest rates rise or if an issuer is unable or unwilling to make principal or interest payments. It's possible that an active trading market for fund shares will not develop, which may hurt your ability to buy or sell fund shares, particularly in times of market stress. Trading securities actively can increase transaction costs, therefore lowering performance and taxable distributions. Foreign investing, especially in emerging markets, has additional risks, such as currency and market volatility and political and social instability. Liquidity—the extent to which a security may be sold or a derivative position closed without negatively affecting its market value, if at all—may be impaired by reduced trading volume, heightened volatility, rising interest rates, and other market conditions. Master limited partnerships (MLPs) holding fixed-income securities such as bonds may be subject to interest-rate and credit risk, potentially causing the partial or complete loss of principal. MLPs are typically concentrated in specific industries and may lack the benefit of diversification. The use of hedging and derivatives could produce disproportionate gains or losses and may increase costs. Fund distributions generally depend on income from underlying investments and may vary or cease altogether in the future. Investments in higher-yielding, lowerrated securities include a higher risk of default. Shares may trade at a premium or discount to their NAV in the secondary market. These variations may be greater when markets are volatile or subject to unusual conditions. data.title: Kinder Morgan: $60 Crude Oil Price Cannot Last
data.description:
KMI Stock: Previous Thesis and New Developments

My last piece for Kinder Morgan, Inc. (NYSE:KMI) was published by Seeking Alpha about 2 months ago on 3.28.2025. That article was titled "Kinder Morgan: Let Your Profit Run" and rated the stock as a buy after examining its FY Q4 2024 earnings report (ER). Quote:

KMI's FY Q4 earnings report ("ER") revealed some headwinds in the near term, ranging from uneven throughputs to cost pressure. However, the overall picture is bright in my view. Judging by the update in the ER, I consider the margin expansion potential and capital expansion projects as the dominant forces. Its current P/E can be well justified by these positive forces, and the cost headwinds are ultimately temporary.

Since then, a few new developments have surfaced and call for a revisit of KMI stock. The rest of this article will focus on the change oil prices (a macroeconomic parameter) and also some company-specific updates provided in its recent Q1 ER for FY 2025. As you can see from the following chart, KMI announced the ER on 4.16, posting EPS that are slightly below the Street's estimates. Revenues, on the other hand, expanded to $4.24 billion and surpassed expectation. The key driver is the company's higher level of natural gas transported, and I will elaborate on this driver later.

The key profit headwind (and also the key cause of seesawing stock prices since my last writing) in my mind is the compressed energy prices oil price. To wit, the following chart shows the price of crude oil futures (CL1:COM). As seen, crude oil price has been under pressure in the past year and have suffered a sharp decline since my last writing as the trade disputed started. The future price dipped to as low as $55 in early May and has slightly recovered to the current level of $60 only.

In the following sections, I will explain A) why I don't think such a low price can last, sustain, and B) why KMI is well-positioned to benefit when/if energy demand and pricing environments improve. These considerations have also led me to reiterate my buy rating for KMI.

Why I Expect Oil Demand and Price to Rise

I expect oil demand and price to rise in the near future for several reasons. To limit the length of this piece and present my arguments more tightly, I will only elaborate on the top two reasons in my model: the refilling of the U.S. strategic petroleum reserve (SPR) and also the relatively low U.S. oil inventory.

For the first reason, the next chart, provided by EIA data, shows the level of the U.S. SPR on a weekly basis. The latest data point is for the week of May 16 as seen, with an ending stocks of 400,493 thousand barrels. For readers unfamiliar with the background, the Biden administration ordered the release of SPR to curb energy price surges shortly after the breakout of the COVID pandemic. This has caused the SPR levels to drop sharply from the pre-pandemic level of ~ 700k barrels to as low as ~348k in mid-2023. Since then, the U.S. has been gradually refilling the SPR as seen. However, the latest SPR reading is still near a record low (that dates as far back as to the 1980s) and I expect the refilling to keep going for a while, thus creating a sustained demand for oil.

As for the second reason, the next chart, provided by YCharts data, shows the level of the U.S. Crude Oil Stocks also on a weekly basis. The latest data point indicates a stock of 843.65 million barrels. The data also exhibits a sharp decrease during the pandemic, and the current level has a long way to recovery to the pre-pandemic level of around $1.2 billion barrels.

KMI Stock: Growth Potential and Profitability

Looking ahead, I expect recovery of energy price and also demand for the above reasons. I further consider KMI is well-positioned to benefit when/if this happens. As aforementioned, KMI reported a nice revenue expansion (better than expected) in the past quarter despite compressed energy prices. The key driver in my model was the higher level of natural gas transported due to greater residential and commercial usage. Additionally, the volumes transported to LNG facilities were also up 15%. I expect the robust volume to further improve in the near future, which could magnify the benefits should my above projection of more favorable pricing environments and demand materialize. Kinder closed the acquisition of a $640 million Bakken gathering and processing system recently, which ought to show improved throughput immediately. In the meantime, KMI also reported progress on several large capital expansion projects (with the Elba expansion as a notable example), which should also support volume throughput.

The stock is currently priced at P/E ratios that are substantially above the median sector and also its own historical averages, as you can clearly see from the screenshot below. However, I believe such a valuation premium is justifiable given the above growth potential and also KMI's superior profitability metrics. As seen from the second table, KMI's current profit margins are vastly better than the sector median and also systematically better than its historical averages.

Other Risks and Final Thoughts

In terms of the negatives, there are a few profit headwinds worth mentioning besides the challenging energy prices. In tandem with the higher gross profits reported in the ER, maintenance and depreciation costs were also much higher in the past quarter. Looking ahead, I expect maintenance and depreciation expenses to remain elevated and could cause some pressure on its margins in the near future. Consensus projections seem to imply the same. As seen in the chart below, KMI's implied net profit margin, based on the EPS and revenues reported in Q1 ER, was 17.9% for the latest quarter on a normalized basis. Looking ahead to the upcoming quarter (with an announcement date expected on July 16, 2025), the consensus forecast points to a normalized EPS of $0.27 and an estimated revenue of $3.72 billion. These numbers translate into an implied net margin of 16.1% under the current share count, indicating a margin compression of around 170 basis points.

To recap, this article is focused on 2 developments surrounding KMI since my last writing: the change in crude oil price and company-specific updates reported in its Q1 ER. After examining these changes, my conclusion is that the stock keeps offering a skewed return/risk profile. In particular, I see multiple catalysts ongoing that can drive up both oil demand and prices. Moreover, I see KMI well poised to benefit should this happen and reiterate my buy rating.
